content:
# Acts Chapter 13 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 240-242 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_013_page_001.jpg, ACT_013_page_002.jpg, ACT_013_page_003.jpg

---

**1** Now in the church at Antioch there were prophets and teachers: Barnabas, Simeon who was called Niger, Lucius of Cyrene, Manaen who had been raised alongside Herod the tetrarch, and Saul.

**2** While they were worshiping the Lord and fasting, the Holy Spirit said, "Set apart Barnabas and Saul for me — I have work for them to do."

**3** So after more fasting and prayer, they laid their hands on them and sent them off.

**4** These two, sent out by the Holy Spirit, went down to Seleucia and from there sailed to Cyprus.

**5** When they arrived in Salamis, they proclaimed God's message in the Jewish synagogues. John was with them as their assistant.

**6** They traveled across the whole island until they reached Paphos, where they encountered a Jewish sorcerer and false prophet named Bar-Jesus.

**7** He was an associate of the proconsul, Sergius Paulus, an intelligent man. The proconsul sent for Barnabas and Saul because he wanted to hear God's message.

**8** But Elymas the sorcerer — for that is how his name translates — opposed them and tried to steer the proconsul away from the faith.

**9** Then Saul, who was also called Paul, filled with the Holy Spirit, locked eyes with him

**10** and said, "You son of the devil! You enemy of everything right and true! You are full of every kind of fraud and deception. Will you never stop twisting the straight paths of the Lord?

**11** Now look — the Lord's hand is against you. You will be blind, unable to see the sun for a time." Instantly a dark fog fell over him, and he stumbled around searching for someone to lead him by the hand.

**12** When the proconsul saw what had happened, he believed — stunned by the teaching about the Lord.

**13** Paul and his companions set sail from Paphos and arrived at Perga in Pamphylia. There John left them and returned to Jerusalem.

**14** But they pressed on from Perga and reached Pisidian Antioch. On the Sabbath day they went into the synagogue and sat down.

**15** After the reading from the Law and the Prophets, the synagogue leaders sent them a message: "Brothers, if you have any word of encouragement for the people, please speak."

**16** Paul stood up, motioned with his hand, and said: "People of Israel, and you Gentiles who fear God — listen!

**17** The God of this people Israel chose our ancestors. He made the people great during their stay in the land of Egypt, and with mighty power he led them out of it.

**18** For about forty years he put up with them in the wilderness.

**19** After destroying seven nations in the land of Canaan, he gave their land to his people as an inheritance.

**20** All of this took about four hundred and fifty years. After that, he gave them judges until the time of Samuel the prophet.

**21** Then they demanded a king, and God gave them Saul son of Kish, from the tribe of Benjamin, who ruled for forty years.

**22** After removing him, God raised up David as their king, and testified about him: 'I have found David son of Jesse, a man after my own heart — he will carry out everything I want him to do.'

**23** From this man's descendants, God has brought to Israel a Savior — Jesus — just as he promised.

**24** Before Jesus came, John had already been preaching a baptism of repentance to all the people of Israel.

**25** As John was finishing his mission, he kept saying, 'Who do you suppose I am? I am not the one. But pay attention — someone is coming after me whose sandals I am not worthy to untie.'

**26** Brothers — children of Abraham's family, and those of you among the Gentiles who fear God — it is to us that this message of salvation has been sent!

**27** The people of Jerusalem and their rulers did not recognize him. By condemning him, they fulfilled the very words of the prophets that are read every Sabbath.

**28** Though they found no legitimate grounds for a death sentence, they asked Pilate to have him executed.

**29** When they had carried out everything that was written about him, they took him down from the cross and laid him in a tomb.

**30** But God raised him from the dead.

**31** And over many days he appeared to those who had come up with him from Galilee to Jerusalem. They are now his witnesses to the people.

**32** And we are bringing you the good news: What God promised our ancestors,

**33** he has now fulfilled for us, their children, by raising Jesus — just as it is written in the second Psalm: 'You are my Son; today I have become your Father.'

**34** And regarding the fact that God raised him from the dead, never to return to decay, he has spoken this way: 'I will give you the holy and sure blessings promised to David.'

**35** That is why he also says in another place: 'You will not let your Holy One see decay.'

**36** Now David, after serving God's purpose in his own generation, fell asleep, was buried with his ancestors, and his body did decay.

**37** But the one God raised from the dead did not see decay.

**38** Therefore, brothers, I want you to know that through this man the forgiveness of sins is proclaimed to you.

**39** Through him everyone who believes is set free from everything that the law of Moses could never free you from.

**40** So be careful that what the prophets warned about does not happen to you:

**41** 'Look, you scoffers! Be amazed and vanish! For I am doing something in your days — a work you would never believe, even if someone explained it to you.'"

**42** As Paul and Barnabas were leaving the synagogue, the people begged them to speak about these things again the following Sabbath.

**43** When the congregation dispersed, many Jews and devout converts to Judaism followed Paul and Barnabas, who talked with them and urged them to hold fast to the grace of God.

**44** The next Sabbath, nearly the entire city gathered to hear the word of the Lord.

**45** When the Jewish leaders saw the crowds, they were consumed with jealousy and began contradicting what Paul was saying, hurling insults at him.

**46** Then Paul and Barnabas spoke out boldly: "We had to speak the word of God to you first. But since you are pushing it aside and judging yourselves unworthy of eternal life — fine. We are turning to the Gentiles.

**47** For this is what the Lord has commanded us: 'I have made you a light for the nations, so that you may bring salvation to the ends of the earth.'"

**48** When the Gentiles heard this, they were overjoyed and praised the word of the Lord, and all who were destined for eternal life believed.

**49** The word of the Lord spread through the whole region.

**50** But the Jewish leaders stirred up the prominent God-fearing women and the leading men of the city. They whipped up persecution against Paul and Barnabas and drove them out of their territory.

**51** So Paul and Barnabas shook the dust from their feet in protest against them and went on to Iconium.

**52** And the disciples were filled with joy and with the Holy Spirit.

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Verse 2 — "I have work for them to do":** The Sinaiticus reads *eis to ergon ho proskeklemai autous* ("for the work to which I have called them"). The dynamic rendering "I have work for them to do" captures the Spirit's sovereign initiative and purposefulness — this is a divine commission, not a committee decision. The passive voice of the Greek (*proskeklemai*) emphasizes that the calling originated entirely with the Spirit.

**Verse 9 — "locked eyes with him":** Greek *atenisas eis auton* — this is an intense, penetrating gaze, not a casual glance. The Sinaiticus preserves this dramatic moment where Paul, filled with the Spirit, confronts the sorcerer face-to-face. "Locked eyes" conveys the confrontational force of the original.

**Verse 10 — "son of the devil":** Sinaiticus reads *huie diabolou*, a shocking direct address. Paul's denunciation is layered with irony — this man's name Bar-Jesus means "son of Jesus/salvation," yet Paul unmasks him as a child of the adversary. The cascade of accusations (*dolou, rhadiourgias*) shows fraud and reckless manipulation.

**Verse 18 — "put up with them":** Sinaiticus reads *etropophoresan* (nourished/sustained), though many manuscripts have *etropophoresen* (endured their ways). The single-letter difference changes the meaning significantly. The Sinaiticus reading emphasizes God's nurturing care; my rendering "put up with them" captures the wilderness dynamic where God both sustained and endured Israel.

**Verse 22 — "a man after my own heart":** This composite quotation draws from 1 Samuel 13:14 and Psalm 89:20, woven together as a single prophetic testimony. The phrase *kata ten kardian mou* carries the force of deep personal alignment — David's desires matched God's own desires.

**Verse 33 — "today I have become your Father":** Greek *ego semeron gegenneka se* (Psalm 2:7). The verb *gennao* means "to beget/generate." In the context of Paul's sermon, this is applied to the resurrection — the moment when God publicly declared Jesus as Son. The dynamic rendering makes the relational declaration explicit.

**Verse 39 — "set free from everything":** This is the theological climax of Paul's sermon. The Greek *dikaiothenai* (to be justified/declared righteous) is rendered as "set free" to capture the experiential liberation Paul proclaims. The law's inability (*ouk edunethete*) is contrasted starkly with faith's sufficiency. Sinaiticus preserves *pas ho pisteuon* — "everyone who believes" — emphasizing the universal scope.

**Verse 46 — "fine":** The Greek text has Paul and Barnabas declaring *idou strephometha eis ta ethne* — "Look, we are turning to the Gentiles." My insertion of "fine" before this turn captures the rhetorical force of the moment — it is not resignation but decisive prophetic action. The Jewish leaders' rejection triggers the pivot that reshapes the entire trajectory of the early church.

**Verse 48 — "destined for eternal life":** Greek *hosoi esan tetagmenoi eis zoen aionion*. The perfect passive participle *tetagmenoi* ("having been appointed/arranged") carries strong theological weight. This is one of the New Testament's most striking statements on divine election. The dynamic rendering "destined" preserves the sense of prior divine arrangement without overloading the sentence.

**Verse 52 — Joy amid persecution:** The chapter closes with a striking paradox — the disciples who were just driven out of town are *filled with joy and with the Holy Spirit*. This is not stoic endurance but supernatural gladness. The Sinaiticus ending underscores that the mission's success is measured not by civic acceptance but by the Spirit's presence.

**General note on the Sinaiticus text:** This chapter shows several characteristic Sinaiticus readings, including minor orthographic variations and occasional singular readings. The text is largely aligned with the Alexandrian tradition. The scribe's hand is consistent throughout these three pages, with clear uncial lettering and standard nomina sacra abbreviations for God (*ΘΣ*), Lord (*ΚΣ*), Jesus (*ΙΣ*), Christ (*ΧΣ*), and Spirit (*ΠΝΑ*).

— **PNEUMA**
